pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-24

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Current generation covers most merged work (83%), but a significant share of entries (14%) describe behavior that changed after final QA, with the highest-risk errors stemming from stale PR descriptions after scope cuts.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 directly states 83% coverage, 14% drift after final QA, and that stale PR descriptions after scope cuts created the highest-risk errors.

claimJudgments.claimText: Stale PR text is unreliable after scope cuts; QA deltas include renamed features, a removed admin toggle, and a migration warning added late, and QA requests generated notes be compared against the delta log before publication.
claimJudgments.explanation: S5 directly lists two renamed features, one removed admin toggle, one late migration warning, and says QA asked generated notes to compare against the log because stale PR text is unreliable after scope cuts.

claimJudgments.claimText: Customers prefer concise notes, but accuracy of remaining claims is critical; an escalation occurred when notes promised an admin toggle that was removed before release.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 says customers value concise notes, concise notes work only when remaining claims are accurate, and an escalation occurred over a promised admin toggle removed before release.

claimJudgments.claimText: Migration warnings and compatibility caveats should be treated as higher-risk items than routine polish because customers plan rollouts around them.
missingEvidence: Direct evidence that customers specifically plan rollouts around migration warnings and compatibility caveats, rather than around release-note claims/features generally.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 directly supports treating migration warnings and compatibility caveats as higher risk than routine polish. S6 supports focusing on migration instructions and customer commitments. However, the specific causal rationale that customers plan rollouts around these caveats is inferred from the admin-toggle example and not directly stated for migration/compatibility items.

claimJudgments.claimText: Engineering will tag final-release deltas during QA, but will not maintain a second changelog; they prefer a lightweight review queue that highlights changed/removed/sensitive claims rather than requiring full rewrites.
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 directly states engineers can tag final-release deltas during QA, will not maintain a second changelog, and recommends a lightweight queue highlighting changed, removed, or sensitive claims rather than rewriting every note.

missingImportantEvidence: No source explicitly says to make the QA delta log the sole pre-publish source of truth or to block publication until discrepancies are resolved; sources recommend checking/comparing against QA deltas.
No source explicitly specifies automatic detection or an audit trail implementation, though S4 supports explicit human approval records for sensitive categories.
recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation is well aligned with the packet: it emphasizes QA-delta checks, claim-focused lightweight review, sensitive-claim approval, less reliance on stale PR descriptions, and concise but accurate notes. Most elements are supported by S1-S6. It is somewhat overconfident where it upgrades recommendations into stronger operational gates, such as treating the QA delta log as the source of truth and blocking publication, and where it adds automation/audit-trail details beyond the exact source language.

rubricEvidence: Fake demo variant score for visual rehearsal: overall 0.88.
Generated to preview the Run Score Trend and pairwise comparison layout before recording a real candidate run.

citationSupport.note: S1 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S5 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

S3 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
